does: This paper identifies and addresses four fundamental inadequacies in current LLM evaluation frameworks—distributional, temporal, scope, and process invalidity—that become particularly problematic for deployed agentic systems. The authors propose Grounded Continuous Evaluation (GCE), a new framework instantiated as ISOPro, which replaces learned reward models with deterministic verifiers to eliminate reward hacking by construction in verifiable domains, and substitutes LoRA adapter updates on CPU instead of dual-model RLHF to reduce hardware barriers by an order of magnitude. The work validates ISOPro across three model architectures (Qwen 2.5 3B, Llama 3.2 3B, Gemma 2 2B) and two domains (scheduling and MBPP programming tasks), demonstrating superior capability gains in matched-compute comparisons against GRPO-LoRA baseline.
connects: This work responds to longstanding issues in reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) pipelines, particularly reward model pathologies and the tendency toward reward hacking that emerge from misaligned learning signals. It builds on critiques of static benchmark-based evaluation, connecting to broader discussions about evaluation validity in machine learning and the inadequacy of offline metrics for dynamic deployment scenarios. The paper extends prior work on verifiable reward structures and lightweight fine-tuning approaches like LoRA, while engaging with recent advances in RLHF variants (GRPO) and agentic LLM deployment. It also connects to literature on reproducibility barriers in large model training, addressing hardware scaling issues that plague contemporary RLHF research.
enables: This framework opens pathways toward more reliable evaluation and training of autonomous LLM agents by establishing verifiable reward mechanisms that prevent gaming and support continuous feedback loops in deployment. ISOPro's reduction of hardware requirements broadens access to RLHF research and enables more researchers to develop safer agentic systems with reproducible results. The deterministic verifier approach could extend to additional verifiable domains beyond scheduling and code generation, potentially informing new verification methods for reasoning tasks. The paper positions future work on temporal evaluation dynamics—how agent behavior shifts across interaction sequences—and scope validity in multi-task agentic scenarios, laying groundwork for evaluation frameworks tailored to real-world deployment constraints and continuous learning environments.
